lessons.title: Terminal & Git Basics
objectives: Navigate the file system using command-line commands
Initialize a Git repository and make commits
Explain why version control matters for AI development

exercises.title: Your First Git Repository
hints: Start by using mkdir to create the project directory, then cd into it.
After creating the directory, run git init to turn it into a repository.
Use echo to create a file, git add to stage it, and git commit -m to save your snapshot.

lessons.title: Python Environment Setup
objectives: Install Python 3.11+ and verify the installation
Create and activate a virtual environment
Install packages with pip and run Python scripts

exercises.title: Python Hello World with Functions
hints: Remember that Python variables don't need type declarations — just assign a value with =.
F-strings let you embed variables inside curly braces: f"Hello, {name}!"
Your greet function should take a name parameter and return an f-string greeting.

lessons.title: VS Code & AI Tools Setup
objectives: Set up VS Code with essential extensions for AI development
Install Ollama and pull a local language model
Send a prompt to a local AI model and receive a response

exercises.title: Talk to a Local AI
hints: The Ollama API lives at http://localhost:11434/api/generate — use httpx.post() to call it.
The request body needs two keys: "model" (e.g. "tinyllama") and "prompt" (your question).
Set "stream" to false in the request body so you get the full response in one shot.

lessons.title: Variables, Types & Functions
objectives: Use Python variables with int, float, str, and bool types
Write functions with type hints, default arguments, and docstrings
Apply string manipulation and mathematical operations in functions

exercises.title: Temperature, Palindromes & BMI
hints: Remember the Fahrenheit formula: F = C * 9/5 + 32. For palindromes, think about how to normalize the text first.
For is_palindrome, use text.lower().replace(" ", "") to clean the input. Then compare the cleaned string to its reverse using [::-1] slice notation.

celsius_to_fahrenheit: return celsius * 9 / 5 + 32. is_palindrome: cleaned = text.lower().replace(" ", ""); return cleaned == cleaned[::-1]. calculate_bmi: return round(weight_kg / (height_m ** 2), 1).

lessons.title: Data Structures
objectives: Use lists, dictionaries, tuples, and sets effectively
Write list comprehensions to transform and filter data
Build a CRUD application using lists of dictionaries

exercises.title: Contact Book
hints: Each contact is a dictionary with 'name' and 'email' keys. The contacts list stores these dicts. Use .append() to add and a for loop to search.
For find_contact, loop through the list and compare contact['name'].lower() to name.lower(). For list_contacts, try a list comprehension with an f-string.
add_contact: contacts.append({'name': name, 'email': email}). find_contact: loop and compare .lower(). remove_contact: use enumerate() to get the index, then contacts.pop(i).

lessons.title: Working with Files & APIs
objectives: Read and write files using Python's open() and with statements
Parse and generate JSON data with the json module
Make HTTP requests with httpx and handle errors with try/except

exercises.title: Fetch and Save JSON
hints: For fetch_and_save, use httpx.get(url, timeout=10) and then response.json() to get the data. For load_json, wrap your file reading in a try/except block.
After getting the response, call response.raise_for_status() before parsing JSON. To save, use json.dump(data, f, indent=2) inside a with open(filepath, 'w') block.

fetch_and_save: response = httpx.get(url, timeout=10); response.raise_for_status(); data = response.json(); then json.dump(data, f, indent=2). load_json: try open and json.load, except FileNotFoundError return None.

lessons.title: What is AI? Machine Learning Basics
objectives: Explain the difference between AI, Machine Learning, and Deep Learning
Identify supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning problems
Describe the high-level steps of the machine learning process

exercises.title: Classify ML Problems
hints: Convert the description to lowercase with .lower(), then check if any keywords from each category appear in the text.
Create three lists of keywords — one for each ML type. Loop through each list and check if any keyword is found.
supervised_keywords = ['predict', 'classify', 'label', 'diagnose', 'spam']. Loop: for kw in supervised_keywords: if kw in text: return 'supervised'.

lessons.title: How LLMs Work
objectives: Explain how text is broken into tokens and why token counts matter
Describe the transformer architecture and attention mechanism at a high level
Understand temperature, top-p, and context windows and their effects

exercises.title: Simple Tokenizer
hints: For simple_tokenize, the regex pattern r"\w+|[^\w\s]" matches either word characters or individual punctuation.
count_tokens just calls simple_tokenize and returns the length with len().
simple_tokenize: return re.findall(r"\w+|[^\w\s]", text). count_tokens: return len(simple_tokenize(text)).

lessons.title: AI Limitations & Ethics
objectives: Identify and explain AI hallucinations, bias, and prompt injection
Describe strategies for mitigating common AI failure modes
Determine when AI is and is not appropriate for a given task

exercises.title: AI Fact Checker
hints: Loop through each claim, then for each claim loop through known_facts.items(). Compare using .lower().
Check if the fact value matches the claim (verified), then if the topic appears but doesn't match (contradicted).
for claim in claims: claim_lower = claim.lower(). For each (topic, fact): if fact.lower() in claim_lower: verified.

lessons.title: Calling LLM APIs
objectives: Call the Ollama chat API with system and user messages
Build a reusable Python class for LLM communication
Handle multi-turn conversations by managing message history

exercises.title: OllamaChat Client
hints: The OllamaChat class needs __init__, send, and send_with_history methods. Store model and host in __init__.
In send(), build a messages list: start empty, optionally append a system message, then append the user message.
send: messages = []; if system_prompt: messages.append({'role':'system','content':system_prompt}); messages.append({'role':'user','content':prompt}).

lessons.title: Building a CLI AI Assistant
objectives: Design system prompts that give an AI assistant personality and constraints
Manage conversation history for multi-turn interactions
Save and load conversations to and from JSON files

exercises.title: CLI Assistant Utilities
hints: create_system_prompt uses an f-string. format_history loops through messages and checks msg['role'].
For format_history, build a list of formatted strings, skip messages where role is 'system', then join with newlines.
create_system_prompt: return f'You are {name}, an AI assistant. {personality} Keep responses concise and helpful.'.

exercises.title: Ex 01
hints: Think about what the `history` list needs to look like before you send the request. Each entry is a dictionary with "role" and "content" keys. You need to append the user's message *before* making the POST request, and the assistant's reply *after* you parse the response.

The API payload should look like this: `{"model": MODEL, "messages": history, "stream": False}`. POST it to `f"{OLLAMA_URL}/api/chat"` using `requests.post(..., json=payload)`. The response JSON has the structure `{"message": {"content": "..."}}` -- extract the content string from there.

Here is near-complete pseudocode for `main()`:
```
print welcome banner
while True:
    try:
        user_input = console.input(prompt).strip()
        if not user_input: continue
        if user_input.lower() == "/quit":
            print goodbye
            break
        reply = chat(user_input)
        console.print(Markdown(reply))
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print goodbye
        break
    except requests.exceptions.ConnectionError:
        print error message
        break
```

For create_few_shot_prompt, loop through examples adding Input/Output pairs, then append the query.
For validate_prompt: estimated_tokens = len(prompt) // 4; check if empty or exceeds max_tokens.

exercises.title: Output Parsers
hints: For extract_json, use re.search with re.DOTALL flag to find JSON objects in text.
For extract_markdown_sections, split on lines starting with # and group content by heading.
For validate_llm_json, call extract_json first, then check required_fields against the parsed data.

lessons.title: Few-Shot Patterns & Chain of Thought

exercises.title: Advanced Prompt Patterns
hints: For build_chain_of_thought_prompt, wrap the question with 'Let's think step by step' instruction.
For extract_final_answer, search for markers like 'therefore' or 'final answer:' in the response.
For build_self_consistency_prompts, create n slightly varied prompts using different reasoning prefixes.

lessons.title: What Are Embeddings?
objectives: Explain what vector embeddings are and why they matter for AI
Compute cosine similarity and euclidean distance between vectors
Find the most similar item in a collection using vector math

exercises.title: Embedding Utilities
hints: For cosine_similarity, compute dot product, magnitude_a, and magnitude_b. Return dot / (mag_a * mag_b).
For euclidean_distance, use math.sqrt(sum((a-b)**2 for a,b in zip(vec_a, vec_b))).
For most_similar, loop through vectors computing cosine_similarity and track the best match.

lessons.title: Vector Databases
objectives: Explain why vector databases are needed for similarity search
Build a simple in-memory vector store with CRUD operations
Implement nearest-neighbor search using cosine similarity

exercises.title: Simple Vector Store
hints: Store documents in a dict: self._store[id] = {'vector': vector, 'metadata': metadata or {}}.
For search(), compute cosine_similarity for each document, sort by score descending, return top_k.
For delete(), use self._store.pop(id, None) to safely remove without KeyError.

lessons.title: Building Semantic Search
objectives: Build an end-to-end semantic search pipeline with embeddings
Implement keyword search as a fallback strategy
Combine semantic and keyword scores in a hybrid search approach

exercises.title: Semantic Search Engine
hints: Store embedding_fn and use an internal dict as the vector store. Index documents by embedding each text.
For keyword_search, split query into terms, count matching terms per document, score = matches / total terms.
For hybrid_search, combine alpha * semantic_score + (1-alpha) * keyword_score for each document.

lessons.title: RAG Architecture
objectives: Explain the Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipeline and its components
Build context from retrieved documents within token limits
Construct augmented prompts that combine context with user queries

exercises.title: RAG Pipeline
hints: Store retriever and generator callables in __init__. build_context joins docs within max_chars limit.
query() chains: retrieve → build_context → build_prompt → generate. query_with_sources adds source tracking.

lessons.title: Document Chunking & Processing
objectives: Implement multiple chunking strategies for different document types
Handle overlapping chunks to preserve context across boundaries
Add metadata to chunks for source tracking and filtering

exercises.title: Document Chunking
hints: For chunk_by_size, use a while loop advancing by (chunk_size - overlap) each step.
For chunk_by_paragraphs, split on '\n\n' and merge small paragraphs until reaching max_chunk_size.
For chunk_markdown, split on heading patterns and handle sections that exceed max_chunk_size.

lessons.title: Building a RAG Pipeline
objectives: Build a complete document QA system from loading to answering
Implement document processing with chunking and embedding
Evaluate RAG answer quality using keyword-based metrics

exercises.title: Document QA System
hints: DocumentQA stores embedding_fn, generate_fn, and an internal list as the vector store.
process_documents chunks text, embeds each chunk, and stores {embedding, text, source}.
answer() retrieves top chunks, joins their text as context, builds prompt, generates, returns with sources.

exercises.title: Ex 01
hints: Start with `chunk_text()` -- it is the simplest function. Use a list comprehension to slice the text at every `chunk_size` interval: `text[i:i+chunk_size]`. Then filter out any chunks where `.strip()` is empty. For `ingest_folder()`, use `Path(folder_path).glob("**/*.md")` to find files and remember to generate unique IDs for each chunk.

For `ingest_folder()`, the ChromaDB `collection.add()` call expects three keyword arguments: `documents` (list of chunk strings), `ids` (list of unique ID strings like `f"{filename}_chunk_{i}"`), and `metadatas` (list of dicts like `{"source": filename}`). For `ask()`, use `collection.query(query_texts=[question], n_results=n)` and access `results["documents"][0]` and `results["metadatas"][0]`.

Here is the near-complete flow:
```python
# chunk_text
chunks = [text[i:i+chunk_size] for i in range(0, len(text), chunk_size)]
return [c for c in chunks if c.strip()]

# ingest_folder
for filepath in Path(folder_path).glob("**/*.md"):
    text = filepath.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    chunks = chunk_text(text)
    ids = [f"{filepath.name}_chunk_{i}" for i in range(len(chunks))]
    metadatas = [{"source": filepath.name} for _ in chunks]
    collection.add(documents=chunks, ids=ids, metadatas=metadatas)

# ask
results = collection.query(query_texts=[question], n_results=n)
context = "\n\n".join(results["documents"][0])
prompt = f"Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}\n\nAnswer based only on the context."
return chat(prompt)
```

run() loops: generate → parse_action → if None return answer, else execute_tool → repeat.

create_app() returns a FastAPI() instance with @app.get and @app.post decorators for endpoints.
For /predict: confidence = min(len(text)/100, 1.0). For /batch: process each text in a list comprehension.

exercises.title: Async LLM Client
hints: AsyncLLMClient uses httpx.AsyncClient for async HTTP calls to the Ollama API.
For batch_generate, use asyncio.Semaphore to limit concurrent requests.
retry_with_backoff loops max_retries times, sleeping base_delay * 2^attempt on failure.

lessons.title: Production Patterns
objectives: Implement rate limiting to protect AI services from overload
Build a TTL-based response cache to reduce redundant LLM calls

exercises.title: Production Middleware
hints: RateLimiter stores timestamps per client_id. Clean old timestamps before checking the count.
ResponseCache uses SHA-256 hashing for keys and checks timestamp + ttl for expiry.
sanitize_input strips control characters and enforces max_length limits.

exercises.title: Ex 01
hints: The chat() helper uses requests.post() to send a JSON payload to f"{OLLAMA_URL}/api/chat". The payload needs three keys: "model" (set to MODEL), "messages" (a list of dicts with "role" and "content"), and "stream" (set to False). The response content is at response.json()["message"]["content"]. Wrap everything in try/except requests.RequestException and raise HTTPException(status_code=503) in the except block.

Each Pydantic model inherits from BaseModel. SummarizeRequest needs one field: text: str. ClassifyRequest needs text: str and categories: list[str]. GenerateRequest needs prompt: str and max_tokens: int = 200 (the default value makes it optional). AIResponse needs result: str. That's it -- Pydantic handles validation automatically.

All three endpoints follow the same pattern: (1) build a system_prompt string with task-specific instructions, (2) call result = chat(system_prompt, req.text) passing the user's input, (3) return AIResponse(result=result). For /classify, join the categories into a string with ', '.join(req.categories) and include them in the system prompt. For /generate, use req.prompt instead of req.text.

objectives: Estimate token counts and track costs across multiple LLM calls

exercises.title: Cost Tracker & Optimizer

select_model picks by task_complexity: 'low' → fastest model, 'high' → highest quality.

exercises.title: Ex 01
hints: For stream_chat(), you need TWO places where streaming is enabled: (1) in the Ollama JSON payload set "stream": True, and (2) in the requests.post() call pass stream=True as a keyword argument. This gives you a response object where you can call response.iter_lines() to get one JSON line at a time. Each line looks like {"message": {"content": "token"}, "done": false}.

The /chat endpoint is surprisingly simple. Import EventSourceResponse from sse_starlette.sse, then return EventSourceResponse(stream_chat(req.message)). That's it -- EventSourceResponse handles the SSE protocol, sets the Content-Type header to text/event-stream, and formats each yielded {"data": token} dict as a proper SSE event.

For the HTML page, use fetch('/chat', {method: 'POST', headers: {'Content-Type': 'application/json'}, body: JSON.stringify({message})}) to send the request. Then read the stream with response.body.getReader() and a TextDecoder. In a while loop, call reader.read() to get chunks. Split each decoded chunk by newlines, and for lines starting with 'data:', extract the token (line.slice(5).trim()) and append it to your message div's textContent.
